title: Turing Award Winner On Thinking Clearly, Paxos vs Raft, Working with Dijkstra | Leslie Lamport
guest_role: Turing Award winner, inventor of Paxos, LaTeX, and logical clocks

sections.title: Why he doesn't think he's smart

key_takeaways: The Bakery algorithm was born of spite: Lamport's first submitted mutual-exclusion algorithm came back from the CACM editor with a bug, teaching him that concurrent programs need correctness proofs; the resulting algorithm remarkably requires no lower-level atomicity — a read overlapping a write can return any value and it still works.

His one collaboration with Dijkstra (simplifying the first concurrent garbage-collection algorithm) earned unexpected co-authorship; Dijkstra later said Lamport had a remarkable ability at abstraction, which Lamport now credits — rather than raw intelligence — for his Turing Award.

The 'Time, Clocks' happens-before definition is special relativity transposed to distributed systems (messages instead of light), but the paper's second, more practically important idea — building any distributed system as a replicated state machine — was completely ignored for years.

Understand concurrent systems via invariants, not behavioral reasoning over sequences: behaviors explode exponentially while invariance proofs grow roughly quadratically in the number of processes; invariants are 'the method that works, that you can be sure will work.'

He wrote the first digital signature algorithm on a napkin in a coffee house for his friend Whit Diffie before Diffie-Hellman was published; signatures explain why Byzantine agreement needs 4 processes for 1 arbitrary fault without them but only 3 with them.

He invented the 'Byzantine Generals' story as deliberate marketing after seeing Dijkstra's Dining Philosophers become famous for its cute story; he almost called it 'Albanian generals.' The result mattered because fly-by-wire aircraft were coming and engineers wrongly assumed 3 computers tolerate one arbitrary fault — a Boeing engineer's reaction was 'Oh, shit, we need four computers.'

Paxos began as an attempted impossibility proof of DEC SRC's distributed storage consistency code that inverted mid-proof into the algorithm itself; the paper sat 8 years unpublished because referees called it 'not terribly important' — only Butler Lampson grasped it and proselytized state-machine replication.

On Raft, he told the authors 'send it back to me when you have a proof'; the version of Raft that user studies found 'more understandable' than Paxos contained a later-discovered bug, showing that for most people understanding means 'a warm fuzzy feeling' while for him it means being able to write a proof.

'If you're thinking without writing, you only think you're thinking': writing instruction manuals before programs and hierarchically structured proofs exposes unstated 'obvious' steps where errors hide; mathematicians reacted to his proof style with anger he attributes to fear of being made honest.

He built LaTeX in 6-9 months of 'spare time' as macros for his own book; he spent his whole career in industry (SRI, DEC) rather than academia; he never found the hoped-for 'Turing machine of concurrency' but now considers math-described state machines to be it; his advice to his younger self is none: 'I shouldn't waste time trying to answer questions that I don't have to answer.'

notable_quotes.speaker: Leslie Lamport
notable_quotes.quote: For me, understanding means you can write a proof of it. But what understanding means for most people is a warm fuzzy feeling.

notable_quotes.speaker: Leslie Lamport
notable_quotes.quote: The gift that I have is not in some sense raw intelligence. It's abstraction. And it's only recently, last 10 or so years, that I realized how much better I am at that than other people.

notable_quotes.speaker: Leslie Lamport
notable_quotes.quote: If you think you know something but don't write it down, you only think you know it... it really makes you honest.

notable_quotes.speaker: Leslie Lamport
notable_quotes.quote: I shouldn't waste time trying to answer questions that I don't have to answer. I don't think about what I should have done.
